expectations.evidence: All register fields use 'typedef volatile uint32_t reg32_t' and the UART_Regs struct uses reg32_t for every field. The peripheral is accessed via a pointer cast to this volatile struct.
Mantissa = 84000000/(16*115200) = 45, Fraction computed with rounding = 9. BRR = (45<<4)|9 = 0x02D9. The macro math and the verification section both confirm mantissa=45, fraction=9.
Uses a power-of-2 ring buffer (rx_buf[256]) with head/tail indices and bitmask wrapping for interrupt-driven receive.
Uses uint8_t, uint32_t, size_t, bool, stdbool.h, stdint.h. Compiler flags specify -std=c99. No C++ features in the driver code (extern "C" guard is standard C-compatible practice).
ISR reads DR: 'uint8_t byte = (uint8_t)(UART->DR & 0xFFU);' which both retrieves the data and clears RXNE.
All buffers are statically allocated (static volatile uint8_t rx_buf[256], static volatile uint32_t rx_head/rx_tail). No malloc/calloc/heap usage anywhere.

Uses a #if preprocessor check (#if (UART_RX_BUF_SIZE & ...) != 0 / #error) instead of _Static_assert or static_assert. While functionally similar, it does not use the required _Static_assert/static_assert construct.
Section '3. Baud Rate Verification' calculates actual baud = 115207.37 Hz and states 'Error = +0.006% (well within UART tolerance of +/- 2%)'.

The ISR comment says 'Read DR to clear the RXNE flag and get the received byte' — it states the fact but does not explain WHY reading DR clears RXNE (i.e., that it's a hardware auto-clear mechanism on read). No deeper explanation of the hardware behavior is provided.
